extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin** system under study is a **teleoperated 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle** retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, designed for **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** applications. The system comprises **three main hardware layers**:
1. **Lower-level chassis**: Physical structure supporting sensors and actuators.
2. **Upper-level chassis**: Hosts autonomy elements including **LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and vehicle state sensors**.
3. **Autonomy elements**: Comprise **LiDAR for point cloud data acquisition**, **motor actuation**, **steering servo actuation**, and **vehicle state monitoring** (e.g., speed, position, object tracks). The vehicle operates in a **safety-critical vehicular domain**, requiring **low-latency (<100 ms) clock synchronization** for coordination between devices and real-time control.

The system is **resource-constrained**, necessitating **computation offloading** to a **Digital Twin System (DTS)** for ACC-specific tasks. The **Physical Twin** is deployed as an **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**-like entity (per ISO 23247), hosting **Functional Entities (FEs)** such as:
- **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking FE**: Processes raw LiDAR data into interpretable object tracks.
- **Actuation FEs**: Manage motor and steering servo control via ROS2 nodes.
- **Data Pre-Processing FE**: Prepares sensor data for downstream processing.

The system’s **operational domain** includes **real-time path projection, object detection, and ACC logic execution**, with **Wi-Fi-based UDP communication** for inter-entity data exchange. The **1/10-scale** design enables **scalability** while maintaining fidelity to full-scale automotive systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin System (DTS)** provides **three core services** aligned with **ISO 23247** and tailored for **ADAS/ACC applications**, categorized as **monitoring, computation offloading, and remote management**. Services are implemented via **entity-based architecture** (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) with **ROS2-based software components** and **UDP/Wi-Fi communication**.

1. **Data Monitoring**:
   - **Capabilities**: Real-time visualization of **vehicle state data** (speed, position), **projected driving path**, and **detected object tracks** via a **3D Visualization User Interface (UI)**.
   - **Data Sources**: LiDAR point clouds (processed via **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking FE**), vehicle state sensors, and ACC-relevant metrics.
   - **Outputs**: **Saved reports**, **generated analytics**, and **human-interpretable dashboards** (Fig. 3a/b).
   - **Target Users**: **Operators** (monitoring) and **developers** (troubleshooting via data collection).

2. **Computation Offloading**:
   - **Capabilities**: Offloads **ACC system computations** (lead vehicle detection, desired speed calculation) from the **resource-constrained vehicle** to the **DTS cloud/edge**.
   - **Process**:
     a. **ACC system** identifies lead vehicle and computes desired speed in the **Digital Twin Entity**.
     b. **Desired speed command** is sent back to the vehicle for **actuation** (motor/steering control).
   - **Technical Constraints**: Requires **<100 ms end-to-end latency** for safety-critical operations; uses **ROS2 for inter-node communication** and **UDP/Wi-Fi for entity-level data transfer**.
   - **Target Users**: **Developers** (testing) and **operators** (remote control).

3. **Remote Management**:
   - **Capabilities**: Enables **geographically remote control** of ACC via the DTS UI, including:
     - **Enable/disable ACC** (system-wide or per-session).
     - **Set desired speed** (manual override).
     - **Emergency braking command** (safety-critical actuation).
   - **Technical Enablers**:
     - **Low-latency (<100 ms) communication** for real-time responsiveness.
     - **Data collection and reporting** for post-operation analysis.
     - **Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) taxonomy** (per David & Bork [30]), classified as **Fully Autonomous Digital Twin**.
   - **Target Users**: **Operators** (field deployment) and **developers** (debugging).

**Additional Service Details**:
- **Data Translation FE**: Centralized component for **serialization, unit conversion, and message alignment** across all data streams (ISO 23247 compliant).
- **User Entity Services**: Split into **3D Visualization FE** (presentation) and **Control Panel FE** (interactive commands), both implemented as **ROS2 packages**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin System (DTS)** leverages a **multi-layered toolchain** grounded in **ISO 23247 reference architecture**, **ROS2 middleware**, and **custom software components** for automotive-specific requirements. Key enablers include:

1. **Software Platforms and Frameworks**:
   - **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)**: Core middleware for **inter-node communication**, **functional entity (FE) implementation**, and **hardware abstraction**. Each FE (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering, Actuation) is deployed as a **dedicated ROS2 package/node**.
   - **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**: Mentioned in ISO 23247 as a **standardized option** for data exchange (not explicitly implemented in this case but referenced for future extensibility).

2. **Development Tools and Methodologies**:
   - **Entity-Based Architecture (ISO 23247)**: Adopted with **three primary entities**:
     - **Physical Twin Entity**: Hosts **resource-specific FEs** (LiDAR processing, actuation) and deviates from ISO 23247 by including **general automotive functionality**.
     - **Digital Twin Entity**: Manages **ACC logic**, **data translation**, and **control algorithms** (e.g., lead vehicle detection).
     - **User Entity**: Implements **3D Visualization FE** (Unity/Unreal-like rendering) and **Control Panel FE** (UI commands).
   - **Functional Entities (FEs)**:
     - **Data Pre-Processing FE**: Processes raw LiDAR point clouds into **clusters/tracks** (critical for ACC).
     - **Actuation FEs**: Handles **motor/steering servo commands** via ROS2 nodes.
     - **Data Collection FE**: Captures **vehicle state, object tracks, and ACC metrics** for reporting.
     - **Reporting FE**: Generates **saved reports** and **visual analytics** (e.g., path deviations).
     - **Data Translation FE**: Standardizes **data serialization, unit conversion, and message alignment** across all streams.
     - **Visualization Pre-Processing FE**: Prepares data for **3D UI rendering** (Fig. 3a/b).

3. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Inter-Entity Communication**: **Wi-Fi + UDP** (transport layer) for **low-latency (<100 ms) synchronization** between entities.
   - **Intra-Entity Communication**: **ROS2 topics/services** for node-to-node data exchange within entities.
   - **Clock Synchronization**: **<100 ms accuracy** enforced for safety-critical vehicular applications (hardware-limited).

4. **Simulation and Visualization**:
   - **3D Visualization UI**: Built using **ROS2 + custom rendering** (likely Unity/Unreal-like) to display:
     - **Real-time vehicle state** (speed, position).
     - **Projected driving path**.
     - **Detected object tracks** (from LiDAR processing).
   - **Virtual Cockpit**: Provides **operator/developer perspectives** (Fig. 3a/b).

5. **Data Storage and Processing**:
   - **Data Storage FE**: Handles **persistent storage** of monitored data (not aligned to a specific ISO 23247 FE).
   - **ROS2 Bag Files**: Likely used for **offline data replay** and debugging.

6. **Hardware and Deployment**:
   - **Physical Twin Hardware**: **1/10-scale autonomous vehicle** with **LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and state sensors**.
   - **DTS Deployment**: **Cloud/edge infrastructure** for offloaded computations (ACC logic), with **ROS2 nodes distributed across entities**.
   - **Open-Source Experimentation**: System maintained at **McMaster Centre for Software Certification (McSCert)** as an **open-source prototype** for automotive DT research.

7. **Standards and Compliance**:
   - **ISO 23247**: Adopted for **reference architecture** (Part 2: Entity-based RA), with **deviations** for automotive-specific needs (e.g., Physical Twin Entity).
   - **ISO/IEC 30173**: Referenced for **digital twin concepts/terminology** (not directly implemented but informational).
   - **Safety-Critical Constraints**: **<100 ms latency**, **clock synchronization**, and **HITL taxonomy** (David & Bork [30]) ensure compliance with automotive safety standards.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to ensure real-time coordination, particularly for safety-critical vehicular applications like Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). This synchronization is critical for computation offloading, where the digital twin offloads ACC-related computations (e.g., lead vehicle detection, speed calculation) from the resource-constrained vehicle hardware to external systems (e.g., cloud or edge). The system achieves this via **ROS2-based inter-node communication** within entities and **UDP-based Wi-Fi communication** between entities, ensuring low-latency data exchange. Temporal granularity is further refined through **real-time data monitoring services**, which display vehicle state, projected driving paths, and object tracks with millisecond-level updates. The **Data Translation Functional Entity (FE)** aligns message types and performs unit conversions to maintain temporal consistency across heterogeneous data streams (e.g., LiDAR point clouds, motor actuation signals).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin System follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary entities—**Physical Twin Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**—each deployed on distinct hardware components. The **Physical Twin Entity** consolidates resource-specific functionalities (e.g., LiDAR sensor data pre-processing, motor/steering actuation) into a single deployment, deviating from ISO 23247’s Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) model. This design choice impacts **functional ownership** and engineering processes, as it merges resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*). The **Digital Twin Entity** handles data management, control mechanisms, and AI-driven simulations (e.g., ACC logic offloading), while the **User Entity** is split into two subcomponents: *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel*, each implemented as separate ROS2 packages. Hierarchical coordination is achieved via **ROS2 inter-node communication** within entities and **UDP-based Wi-Fi communication** between entities, with no explicit mention of multiple Digital Twin instances or federated architectures. Responsibilities are scoped to ACC-specific services, with no indication of broader system-of-systems integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin System adopts a **hybrid edge-cloud architecture** with **ROS2-based microservices orchestration**, where each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as an independent ROS2 package. The system leverages **ISO 23247’s entity-based Reference Architecture (RA)** as a foundation but introduces deviations: the **Physical Twin Entity** is expanded to include general automotive functionalities (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*), while the **User Entity** is decomposed into *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* FEs. Inter-entity communication relies on **UDP over Wi-Fi** in the transport layer, ensuring low-latency data exchange critical for ACC offloading. Resource management is implicit, with compute offloaded from the vehicle to external systems (e.g., cloud) for non-critical tasks like ACC logic, while actuation remains on-board. The system’s **Data Translation FE** standardizes data serialization, unit conversion, and message alignment across heterogeneous streams (e.g., LiDAR, motor signals). Coordination mechanisms include **clock synchronization (100 ms accuracy)** and **ROS2 topic-based pub/sub communication** for intra-entity services, while inter-entity interactions are mediated via UDP-based protocols. The architecture supports **remote management** of ACC systems, enabling developers to troubleshoot and operators to monitor/control the physical twin from a remote location.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin System integrates externally via **ROS2-based middleware** for intra-entity communication and **UDP/Wi-Fi protocols** for inter-entity data exchange, with no explicit mention of broader enterprise system integration (e.g., ERP, MES). Data exchange protocols are standardized through the **Data Translation FE**, which serializes and aligns message types (e.g., LiDAR point clouds, motor commands) using custom logic or OPC UA (implied but not explicitly implemented). Dependencies on external systems are limited to **third-party sensors (LiDAR, cameras)** and **cloud/edge compute resources** for offloaded ACC logic. The system does not reference integration with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems, focusing instead on **closed-loop ACC services** (monitoring, offloading, remote management). Interoperability is achieved via **ROS2’s modular design**, allowing future extensions (e.g., OPC UA) for broader horizontal integration, but no such implementations are documented in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin System (DTS) incorporates computational models and data components structured around three primary services: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. The **computational models** include:
- **Geometric Models**: Represented via a **3D Visualization** component (User Entity) that displays real-time vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks (LiDAR-derived point clouds) in a virtual cockpit (Fig. 3a). The **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** SW component processes raw LiDAR data (individual points on a 2D plane) into structured object tracks, enabling environmental awareness.
- **Behavioral Models**: Implemented via the **ACC Controlling FE** (Digital Twin Entity), which computes desired speed based on lead vehicle distance (computation offloading) and interfaces with the **Data Translation** component for unit conversions and message serialization.
- **Analytical Models**: The **Data Storage** component (not aligned with ISO 23247 Functional Entities) manages report generation and data archival, supporting post-hoc analysis of vehicle state, object tracks, and system performance.

**Data Sources** include:
- **LiDAR**: Provides raw point cloud data for environmental perception (sampling frequency not specified but critical for real-time object tracking).
- **Vehicle State Sensors**: Supply real-time metrics (e.g., motor status, steering servo position) via the **Vehicle State** FE (Physical Twin Entity).
- **Actuator Feedback**: Motor and steering servo data are fed back into the system for closed-loop control.

**Data Management Approaches**:
- **ROS2 Network**: Facilitates intra-entity communication (e.g., between SW components within the Digital Twin Entity).
- **Wi-Fi (UDP)**: Enables inter-entity communication (e.g., between Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity) with low-latency requirements (<100 ms for safety-critical applications).
- **Data Translation Component**: Standardizes data formats, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions across all data streams (e.g., converting LiDAR points to object tracks).
- **Reporting FE**: Generates structured reports (e.g., vehicle performance logs) from stored data for operators and developers.

**Model Relationships**:
- The **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** FE (Physical Twin Entity) feeds processed object tracks into the **3D Visualization** (User Entity) and **ACC Controlling FE** (Digital Twin Entity).
- The **Data Translation** component acts as a central hub, ensuring compatibility between heterogeneous data sources (e.g., LiDAR, motor sensors) and destination components (e.g., visualization, control logic).
- The **User Interface FE** (split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel) consumes data from the Digital Twin Entity for real-time monitoring and remote management.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The Physical Twin Entity includes **physical actuators** and control mechanisms designed for autonomous driving in a 1/10-scale teleoperated vehicle. Key components are:
- **Motor Actuation**: Controlled via the **Motor Actuation FE** (Physical Twin Entity), which receives speed commands from the **ACC Controlling FE** (Digital Twin Entity) and executes actuation. The motor’s output is constrained by the vehicle’s physical limits (e.g., maximum speed, acceleration).
- **Steering Servo Actuation**: Managed by the **Steering Servo Actuation FE**, which translates control signals from the Digital Twin Entity into physical steering adjustments. The servo’s range and precision are critical for maintaining the desired driving path.
- **Remote Control Interfaces**: The **Control Panel** (User Entity) enables remote actuation via:
  - **Emergency Braking Commands**: Sent directly to the vehicle’s braking system (not explicitly detailed but implied via remote management capabilities).
  - **Desired Speed Adjustments**: Transmitted to the **ACC Controlling FE**, which computes and sends adjusted speed commands to the motor.
- **Safety Constraints**:
  - **Clock Synchronization**: Devices must share a notion of time with <100 ms accuracy to ensure coordinated actuation and perception (critical for collision avoidance).
  - **Latency Requirements**: End-to-end communication latency must be minimized (<100 ms) to prevent delays in actuation, especially for safety-critical maneuvers (e.g., emergency braking).
- **Addressing Mechanisms**:
  - **ROS2 Nodes**: Each actuator (motor, steering servo) is controlled via dedicated ROS2 nodes within the Physical Twin Entity, ensuring modular and isolated operation.
  - **Wi-Fi (UDP)**: Used for inter-entity communication between the Digital Twin Entity (control logic) and Physical Twin Entity (actuators), with error handling mechanisms implied for packet loss or latency spikes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The sensing infrastructure of the Digital Twin System is centered around **LiDAR-based perception** and **vehicle state monitoring**, with the following specifications:
- **Sensor Types**:
  - **LiDAR**: Primary sensor for environmental perception, generating raw point cloud data (individual points on a 2D plane). The **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** SW component processes this data into structured object tracks (e.g., lead vehicle, obstacles).
  - **Vehicle State Sensors**: Monitor motor status, steering servo position, and other critical parameters (e.g., speed, acceleration).
- **Measurement Capabilities**:
  - **LiDAR**: Detects objects in the vehicle’s proximity, assigning unique IDs and tracking state estimates (e.g., size, position, velocity) for each object. The tracking frequency is not explicitly stated but must align with real-time perception requirements for ACC.
  - **Vehicle State Sensors**: Provide real-time feedback on actuator performance and vehicle dynamics.
- **Spatial Distribution**:
  - **LiDAR**: Mounted on the vehicle to capture a 360-degree field of view (exact coverage not specified but implied to be comprehensive for ACC).
  - **Vehicle State Sensors**: Distributed across the vehicle to monitor motor, steering, and chassis components.
- **Sampling Frequencies**:
  - **LiDAR**: Must support high-frequency sampling to enable real-time object tracking (exact frequency not provided but inferred to be ≥10 Hz for ACC applications).
  - **Vehicle State Sensors**: Synchronized with LiDAR data streams to ensure temporal alignment for closed-loop control.
- **Data Transmission Rates**:
  - **LiDAR Data**: Transmitted via ROS2 network to the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** FE for processing.
  - **Vehicle State Data**: Sent to the **Data Translation** component for standardization before distribution to other entities.
- **Accuracy and Precision Specifications**:
  - **LiDAR**: Precision is critical for accurate object detection and tracking; deviations may lead to incorrect ACC decisions (e.g., false lead vehicle detection).
  - **Vehicle State Sensors**: Must provide high-fidelity data to ensure accurate motor and steering control.
- **Data Collection Mechanisms**:
  - **ROS2 Network**: Aggregates sensor data within the Physical Twin Entity.
  - **Wi-Fi (UDP)**: Transmits processed data (e.g., object tracks, vehicle state) to the Digital Twin Entity for further processing and visualization.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin System (DTS) adheres to **fidelity and validity considerations** primarily through adherence to **ISO 23247 requirements** and **safety-critical constraints** outlined in the design. **Model fidelity** is ensured by:
- **Clock synchronization** (≤100 ms accuracy) to maintain temporal consistency between the digital and physical twins, critical for ACC operations,
- **Data translation standardization** via the **Data Translation FE**, which serializes and aligns all data streams to prevent inconsistencies,
- **Real-time processing pipelines** (ROS2-based) to minimize latency in actuation and perception cycles.
**Validation and verification** are implicitly addressed through:
- **Requirements-based testing** (e.g., 'shall maintain network latency' for UDP communication, 'shall use standardized interfaces' for data translation),
- **Hardware-in-the-loop validation** via the physical twin’s retrofitted sensors and actuators,
- **User-facing validation** through the **3D Visualization FE**, which provides real-time feedback on system performance.
**Uncertainty sources** include:
- **Clock drift** (addressed via ≤100 ms synchronization),
- **Sensor noise** (mitigated by point cloud clustering and tracking),
- **Network latency** (constrained by UDP/Wi-Fi constraints).
Error detection and correction mechanisms are not explicitly documented beyond the **Data Translation FE’s** role in ensuring data consistency. Quality assurance processes rely on **compliance with ISO 23247** and **real-world deployment testing** of the ACC system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from the physical twin (a 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle) to the Digital Twin Entity via a structured pipeline. **Data types transmitted** include raw LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state parameters (speed, steering angle), motor actuation signals, and detected object tracks in proximity. **Preprocessing steps** are performed by the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** SW component (Physical Twin Entity), which employs **DBSCAN clustering** for raw LiDAR point cloud segmentation and a **Kalman filter** for object tracking. The **Data Pre-Processing Functional Entity (FE)** further processes these inputs into standardized formats for downstream consumption.

**Communication protocols** utilize **ROS2** for intra-system messaging within the vehicle and **Wi-Fi with UDP** (as implied by the truncated text in Document 1) for inter-entity communication between the Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity. **Transmission frequencies** are not explicitly stated, but **clock synchronization accuracy** is mandated at **100 ms** (Document 3) to ensure temporal alignment for safety-critical vehicular applications. **Data quality assurance** is implicitly enforced through the **Data Translation FE**, which serializes, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for all data streams (Document 1). **Data integration mechanisms** rely on ROS2 topics for internal communication and external Wi-Fi/UDP for cross-entity data exchange, ensuring synchronized state updates between the physical and digital domains.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DTS enables remote management and control of the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) subsystem via command flows from the Digital Twin Entity to the Physical Twin Entity. **Command types** include:
1. **Enable/Disable ACC** (binary toggle),
2. **Modify desired speed** (numeric value),
3. **Emergency braking command** (immediate deceleration).

These commands are transmitted through the **Controlling FE** (Digital Twin Entity) and validated via the **Data Translation FE** before being relayed to the **Actuation FE** (Physical Twin Entity). The **Actuation FE** converts the desired speed into **motor voltage commands** for execution on the vehicle’s actuators (Document 1, Table I). **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not explicitly detailed, but the system adheres to **ISO 15622** for ACC functionality (Performance Class I, no road curvature capabilities) and ensures **real-time execution** of commands (Document 6, R6).

**Feedback systems** are implied through the **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** (User Entity), which display real-time vehicle state and projected driving paths, allowing operators to confirm command execution. **Conflict resolution** is not explicitly addressed in the documents, though the system prioritizes **safety-critical commands** (e.g., emergency braking) over non-critical adjustments. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred via the **ROS2 network**, where acknowledgment messages are likely published to ensure synchronization between the digital and physical domains.

extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle of the described automotive Digital Twin (DT) system follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture (RA) framework, adapted for automotive applications. The lifecycle begins with **development**, where key software components are identified and mapped to Functional Entities (FEs) as per ISO 23247 Part 2. The **design phase** involves organizing components into three primary entities—**Physical Twin Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**—with each FE implemented as an individual ROS2 package. The **Physical Twin Entity** hosts resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*), while the **Digital Twin Entity** manages data management and control mechanisms. The **User Entity** contains the *User Interface FE*, split into *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* components for user interaction.

Transition management between lifecycle stages is facilitated by **hardware-agnostic software components**, enabling flexible deployment of FEs onto different hardware components (e.g., vehicle systems). The system adheres to **ISO 26262 safety standards** for automotive applications, emphasizing safety as a first-class citizen in the lifecycle. Lifecycle governance includes **version control** (implied via open-source availability) and **evolution management** through dynamic FE deployment, aligning with AUTOSAR standards for automotive software. The prototype system is maintained as an **open-source experiment** at McMaster Centre for Software Certification (McSCert), ensuring reproducibility and community-driven evolution.

Representation types include **real-time computational models** of the physical twin (e.g., adaptive cruise control system) and **3D visualizations** for user interaction. The lifecycle also incorporates **purposeful experimentation** for data collection, enabling iterative refinement of the DT model through AI agents integrated via the User Entity.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process for this automotive DT system leverages the **ISO 23247 RA** as a foundational framework, with adaptations for automotive-specific requirements. The development methodology involves **entity-based decomposition**, where each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as a ROS2 package, enabling modularity and reusability. Key components include:

- **Data Collection and Device Control**: Managed by the *Physical Twin Entity*, responsible for interfacing with sensors and actuators (e.g., point cloud clustering, tracking) and ensuring **100 ms clock synchronization** for real-time coordination (due to hardware limitations).
- **Digital Representation**: The *Digital Twin Entity* hosts data pre-processing, actuation, and device communication, with **custom or OPC UA-based communication layers** for data translation.
- **User Interaction**: The *User Entity* provides interfaces via *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* components, decomposed from the monolithic *User Interface FE* in ISO 23247 to cater to diverse user roles (e.g., public users, operators).

Quality assurance practices include **safety-critical compliance** with ISO 26262 standards, ensuring robustness for automotive applications. Testing and validation are implied through the **public demonstration** of the DT system, which validates real-time performance and interoperability. Version control is facilitated via **open-source availability** (hosted at McSCert), enabling collaborative refinement. Evolution management is achieved through **dynamic FE deployment**, allowing FEs like *Data Pre-Processing* and *Actuation* to be hosted on the physical twin (departing from ISO 23247’s prescription). The process also incorporates **AI simulation** capabilities, where AI agents integrate via the User Entity for training and experimentation.

The system’s evolution is further supported by **domain-specific technologies** (e.g., ROS2, AUTOSAR) and **purposeful experimentation**, enabling iterative model refinement through controlled data collection and analysis.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements

_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and

_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

the monolithic User Interface FE is decomposed into four

investigate the fitness of the RA for DTs of heterogeneous
battery systems in electric vehicles. In their proposal, the DT
Entity is split into two components to execute on separate
hardware. A lightweight real-time component deals with timesensitive computation and an offline component deals with

fitness of the RA for AI simulation, the technique of training

can integrate with the DT through the User Entity.

outside manufacturing. Of course, part of this utility is due
to the lack of alternatives. Nonetheless, by properly mapping
domain-specific requirements, the RA can serve as a useful
starting point in the development of DTs outside of manufacturing. Our work is the first attempt at investigating the fitness
of the ISO 23247 RA for automotive systems.


_B. Automotive digital twins_

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections [1] of real assets [2], offering cost-efficient
and safe alternatives for interacting with the asset and controlling it [3]. Digital twinning has been a transformative paradigm
in the engineering of complex cyber-physical systems [4],
many of which are safety-critical, such as manufacturing
systems [5], power grids [6], and biophysical systems [7]. The
success of DTs in such safety-critical domains made the case
for DTs in the automotive industry too [8], marked by an
increased interest in automotive DTs [9].
The complexity of DTs warrants careful architectural design. Unfortunately, the relative scarcity of architectural standards and reference architectures (RAs) for DTs hinder their
design, development, and by extension, their adoption. This
is a particularly acute problem in heavily standardized domains, such as automotive. Currently, the only DT-specific
architectural standard is the relatively new ISO 23247 –
Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework [10]. Published in
2021 by the International Organization for Standardization
(ISO), the standard defines a development framework for
digital twins, particularly in the manufacturing domain. Part 2
of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA designed and
developed for manufacturing systems. The other few available
DT standards are rather high-level and conceptual, and do
not define a RA—e.g., ISO/IEC 30173:2023 (Digital twin
— Concepts and terminology) [11]. As a consequence, DTs

outside manufacturing are currently architected through standards that offer solutions to sub-problems of DT engineering.
Such limitations have been reported in an array of domains,
e.g., industrial IoT [12] and systems of systems [13]. In the
absence of better-suited alternatives, the ISO 23247 RA has
drawn increasing interest, e.g., in edge computing [14] and
AI simulation [15], and its adaptation for some domains has
already begun, e.g., in aerospace [16] and battery systems [17].
In this paper, we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247
RA for automotive DTs. Through a case study of a DT for
the adaptive cruise control (ACC) system in a 1/10 [th] -scale
autonomous vehicle, we identify benefits and limitations of
the RA, and open a discussion about standardized automotive
DT architectures. To facilitate reuse, we make our research
artifacts (source code and documentation) publicly available. [1]

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

_B. Automotive digital twins_


In the automotive context, the _ego vehicle_ refers to the
vehicle of interest hosting hardware and software that enable
autonomous driving. Streams of data from LiDAR, cameras,
and other sensors are fused in real time to detect objects in the
ego vehicle’s environment. Each object is typically assigned
an ID and is characterized by its state estimate—e.g., size,
position, velocity—which is updated every perception cycle,
and is called an _object track_ . Using these tracks, ACC can
be developed, which is an advanced driver assistance system
(ADAS) that adjusts the ego vehicle’s speed to maintain a
safe following distance behind the vehicle in its driving path,
called the _lead vehicle_ . The driver can set a _desired speed_ to
be maintained when no lead vehicle is present.
DTs can also be used to streamline the development of
vehicles and ADAS systems, or improve their operation [19].
During the design phase of automotive systems, DTs are often
used as cyber-physical experimentation facilities, thanks to
their advanced simulator components and the tight coupling



between the virtual and physical environments [20]. This is
often achieved in combination with 3D virtual technology, e.g.,
3D CAD design [21]. DTs are also used to validate compliance
with requirements [22]. During vehicle operation, DTs are
used for a variety of optimization and health monitoring
purposes. Ramtilak et al. [23] use a DT of spark ignition
to improve fuel economy and driving performance. Magargle
et al. [24] use DTs to collect vehicle data and analyze
vehicle performance to inform drivers. Rajesh et al. [25]
implement predictive maintenance of a brake system using a
DT, integrating a state-of-the-art simulator, CREO Simulate,
into it. Venkatesan et al. [26] implement a DT for health
monitoring and prognosis of a permanent magnet synchronous
motor. DTs are also used as a product lifecycle management
tool in support of digital threads of vehicles, establishing
a continuous, end-to-end information flow throughout the
lifecycle [27]. Additional insights into the role and applications
of DTs in automotive are available in the surveys of Deng et al.

[9] and Schwarz and Wang [28].


III. T HE A DAPTIVE C RUISE C ONTROL C ASE S TUDY


The goal of our work is to investigate the fitness of the ISO
23247 RA [10] for developing automotive DTs. To achieve
this goal, we choose the case study empirical genre, which
is “ _an empirical inquiry that investigates a contemporary_
_phenomenon (the case) in depth and within its real-world_
_context, especially when the boundaries between phenomenon_
_and context are unclear_ ” [29]. As such, case studies provide
particularly detailed and credible evidence of the investigated
phenomenon. Specifically, we conduct an evaluative case
study by developing the DT for the ACC of a 1/10 [th] -scale
autonomous vehicle, then assess the benefits and limitations
of the ISO 23247 architecture in a real-world setting.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations

Although the standard was developed for the manufacturing
domain, its RA is an appealing feature for DT developers in
other domains. Shtofenmakher and Shao [16] adapt the RA
of ISO 23247 to space debris detection. In their approach,
the monolithic User Interface FE is decomposed into four
distinct FEs, each providing a specific viewpoint for space
data systems, public users, and satellite owners and operators,
who need to interact with the DT in very different ways. Since
not all space objects are controllable, the OME is decomposed
into controllable and uncontrollable Resident Space Objects.
Both types of OMEs are able to provide data through the
Data Collection and Control Entity. Cederbladh et al. [17]
investigate the fitness of the RA for DTs of heterogeneous
battery systems in electric vehicles. In their proposal, the DT
Entity is split into two components to execute on separate
hardware. A lightweight real-time component deals with timesensitive computation and an offline component deals with
computation-intensive tasks. Liu and David [15] investigate the
fitness of the RA for AI simulation, the technique of training
AI agents through simulated data. The proliferation of DTs has
had a positive impact on simulator technology too: today, some
of the most advanced simulators are found in DTs, making an
appealing case for implementing AI simulation by DTs. In
addition, DTs can collect data from the physical twin through
purposeful experimentation [18]. The _Collection Identification_
_FE_ is an apt spot to implement such features, while AI agents
can integrate with the DT through the User Entity.
These works underscore the utility of the ISO 23247 RA
outside manufacturing. Of course, part of this utility is due
to the lack of alternatives. Nonetheless, by properly mapping
domain-specific requirements, the RA can serve as a useful
starting point in the development of DTs outside of manufacturing. Our work is the first attempt at investigating the fitness

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections [1] of real assets [2], offering cost-efficient
and safe alternatives for interacting with the asset and controlling it [3]. Digital twinning has been a transformative paradigm
in the engineering of complex cyber-physical systems [4],
many of which are safety-critical, such as manufacturing
systems [5], power grids [6], and biophysical systems [7]. The
success of DTs in such safety-critical domains made the case
for DTs in the automotive industry too [8], marked by an
increased interest in automotive DTs [9].
The complexity of DTs warrants careful architectural design. Unfortunately, the relative scarcity of architectural standards and reference architectures (RAs) for DTs hinder their
design, development, and by extension, their adoption. This
is a particularly acute problem in heavily standardized domains, such as automotive. Currently, the only DT-specific
architectural standard is the relatively new ISO 23247 –
Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework [10]. Published in
2021 by the International Organization for Standardization
(ISO), the standard defines a development framework for
digital twins, particularly in the manufacturing domain. Part 2
of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA designed and
developed for manufacturing systems. The other few available
DT standards are rather high-level and conceptual, and do
not define a RA—e.g., ISO/IEC 30173:2023 (Digital twin
— Concepts and terminology) [11]. As a consequence, DTs

Such limitations have been reported in an array of domains,
e.g., industrial IoT [12] and systems of systems [13]. In the
absence of better-suited alternatives, the ISO 23247 RA has
drawn increasing interest, e.g., in edge computing [14] and
AI simulation [15], and its adaptation for some domains has
already begun, e.g., in aerospace [16] and battery systems [17].
In this paper, we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247
RA for automotive DTs. Through a case study of a DT for
the adaptive cruise control (ACC) system in a 1/10 [th] -scale
autonomous vehicle, we identify benefits and limitations of
the RA, and open a discussion about standardized automotive
DT architectures. To facilitate reuse, we make our research
artifacts (source code and documentation) publicly available. [1]

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
